summary:
President John F. Kennedy was assassinated on November 22, 1963, while riding in a motorcade through Dealey Plaza in Dallas, Texas. Kennedy was struck by two rifle bullets and was pronounced dead shortly afterward at Parkland Memorial Hospital. Texas Governor John Connally, riding in the same car, was also wounded but survived. Lee Harvey Oswald, a former Marine who had defected briefly to the Soviet Union, was arrested later that day in connection with the killing. Two days later, while being transferred between police facilities, Oswald was shot and killed by Dallas nightclub owner Jack Ruby, in front of live television cameras.

The Warren Commission, established by President Lyndon Johnson to investigate the assassination, concluded in September 1964 that Oswald had acted alone. A subsequent congressional investigation in 1979 reached a different conclusion, suggesting on the basis of acoustic evidence that there had probably been two shooters, implying a conspiracy. The acoustic evidence was later disputed. Decades of conflicting analyses, documentary releases, and competing theories have kept the question of whether Oswald acted alone one of the most debated topics in American history, despite the preponderance of scholarly opinion supporting the lone-gunman conclusion.

Kennedy's assassination was transmitted live to the American public through radio and television in a way that no previous national tragedy had been, and the collective shock created a defining moment of cultural and generational memory. The [[event:2740|television]] coverage of the assassination, the lying-in-state, the funeral, and the murder of Oswald constituted the first major global media event of the television era.

The assassination had significant political consequences. Johnson used the national mourning to secure passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and other Great Society legislation that Kennedy had struggled to advance. Kennedy's presidency has been idealized in American memory to an extent that continues to influence political mythology, while debates over the unfinished nature of his agenda and the meaning of his death continue in historical scholarship.
